lines.text: Alright. We're {{ELAPSED}} into this thing and the night's *weird*, folks. Quiet. Too quiet. Like the Fremen are holding their breath before the sandworm shows up. So let's talk about NOFX, 'Linoleum,' off Punk in Drublic. 1994. The album that peaked at number twelve on the Heatseekers chart and went gold six years later because nobody was paying attention at first.

Fat Mike — Michael Burkett — started this band with Erik Sandin and Eric Melvin back in LA in '83. Fairfax High School. These guys were never supposed to matter. They named themselves after a Boston hardcore band called Negative FX and just *refused* to sign to a major label. Think about that. Refused. In the '90s. While everybody else was chasing majors.

El Hefe joined in '91, added the trumpet, made them *sing*. And then Punk in Drublic drops and it's this perfect storm of sneering guitars and melodic hooks that shouldn't work together. 'Linoleum' is the opening salvo. Fat Mike's voice is like sandpaper on steel. You either get it or you don't.

They made a video for 'Leave It Alone' from that same album and *refused* to send it to MTV. Fat Mike said it straight: 'We just didn't want to be a part of that machine.' That's the punk ethic right there. That's what separates the ones who meant it from the ones who were just wearing the costume.
And here we are in 2169, still playing this record on an unlicensed frequency on a night when the law doesn't exist. NOFX would approve of that. Probably.

Next up: Green Day. 'Basket Case.' Same year as NOFX's moment, 1994, but from a different coast. This is off Dookie, which they recorded in three weeks at Fantasy Studios in Berkeley. Three weeks. Can you imagine? Rob Cavallo produced it. The album that moved 20 million copies worldwide.

Billie Joe Armstrong, Mike Dirnt, Tré Cool. Armstrong's father died when he was ten. Dirnt's mother was a heroin addict — he got adopted by a Native American mother and a white father who divorced when he was seven. These aren't trust-fund kids playing at rebellion. This is lived experience with a hook.

'Basket Case' is about anxiety. About your brain eating itself from the inside. Billie Joe's lyrics touched on boredom, anxiety, masturbation — stuff nobody was *supposed* to sing about in mainstream rock. And then the hook just *lands*. 'Do you have the time to listen to me whine?' Everybody in America screamed that back at him.

The band came out of the late-'80s Bay Area scene around 924 Gilman Street in Berkeley. Punk enough to be real, accessible enough to matter. They forged their own path because nobody else would let them be both things at once.
Still here at midnight. The city's still breathing. Mostly.

The Descendents. 'Hope.' 1982. Off Milo Goes to College. And the title is literal — Milo Aukerman actually *went* to the University of California, San Diego. While the band was happening. This is a band that named their album after a real thing their singer was actually *doing*.

Frank Navetta, Tony Lombardo, Bill Stevenson formed this in Manhattan Beach in 1977 as a power-pop/surf-punk thing. Then Milo showed up in '79 and they became something else entirely — melodic hardcore from the LA scene. By '82, they're recording at Media Art studios and they're the smartest kids in the room.

What made them different — and this is real — they sang about *everything* normal punks didn't sing about. Girls. Food. Parents. Getting rejected. Steven Blush wrote that their 'cheeky love songs disguised as hardcore blasts became the most aped formula in rock.' Beach Boys filtered through punk aggression.

Here's the thing — Navetta left the band during the 'I Don't Want to Grow Up' recording to become a fisherman in Oregon. Tony Lombardo left after because he was a postal carrier and touring didn't work. These guys just *lived*. They didn't sacrifice their actual lives for the band.
'Hope' is quiet. It's a breather. And that's what we need right now. A moment to breathe before the next surge.

Blink-182. 'Dammit.' 1997. Off Dude Ranch. This is the song that broke them wide open. Mark Hoppus on vocals and bass, Tom DeLonge on guitar, Scott Raynor on drums — they met at a Battle of the Bands competition in Poway, California, and decided to change '90s punk forever.

These kids grew up in a San Diego that had an active surfing, skating, and snowboarding scene. It fed the music. They were never going to be serious. They were never going to sing about revolution. They were going to sing about wanting to take a girl to a movie and being too nervous to do it. And somehow that became the most important thing any of them could say.

Mark Hoppus fell from a lamppost in front of Tom DeLonge's house trying to impress him. Broke his ankles. Was on crutches for three weeks. That's the kind of stupid devotion this band was built on. And then there's the time Mark's girlfriend gave him an ultimatum — choose her or the band. He chose the band. Then broke up with her. And came back.

'Dammit' is three minutes of straightforward pop-punk perfection. Simple arrangements. Lyrics about relationships and adolescent frustration. The song that proved you didn't need to be politically conscious or artistically ambitious to matter. You just needed to *mean* it. And they meant it.
The clock reads {{CLOCK}}. Weird night out there. Can feel it.

Dead Milkmen. 'Punk Rock Girl.' 1988. Off Beelzebubba. This song hit MTV in the middle of the '80s and nobody knew what to do with it. Rodney Anonymous, Joe Jack Talcum, Dave Blood, Dean Clean — Philadelphia boys who formed in 1983 out of Garth and Joe Genaro's home-recording project in Chester County. They named the band after a character in Toni Morrison's Song of Solomon. That's the kind of weird literary confidence these guys had.

The Dead Milkmen were never meant to be serious. 'Bitchin' Camaro' got college radio attention because it was *funny*. Jangly punk guitars with thick Philadelphia accents singing about toxic waste and jellyfish afterlifes and UFO conspiracy theories. They were the anti-punk punk band — satirizing the scene while they were inside it.

'Punk Rock Girl' secured MTV rotation in 1988 and they performed it live on Club MTV with complete chaos — improvisational dialogue, stage antics, the whole irreverent mess. It was like watching people have fun in a scene that had mostly forgotten how.

Here's my favorite part — after the big commercial push, they had a deal with Hollywood Records that soured *hard*. So on their 1994 live album, they *smuggled* an unlisted version of 'If I Had a Gun' onto the compilation because the label wouldn't let them. Just snuck it on there. That's punk rock. That's the actual punk rock right there.
And you know what? 'Punk Rock Girl' still works. Still makes you want to find someone weird and embrace the absolute chaos of it all. Which, given the night we're having, seems pretty timely.

tracks.title: My Heart Will Go On
tracks.artist: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes

lines.text: Me First and the Gimme Gimmes. 'My Heart Will Go On.' 1997. These guys formed in San Francisco as a *supergroup cover band* — and I know that sounds stupid, but it's the smartest thing anybody did in the '90s. Spike Slawson, Fat Mike from NOFX, Joey Cape from Lagwagon, Chris Shiflett before Foo Fighters, Dave Raun. These are guys from other bands who just decided to spend their off-time covering other people's songs.

They never intended to release albums. They were just doing covers for fun. But then Fat Mike started Fat Wreck Chords as his label and suddenly the Gimmes had a home. First album was Have a Ball in 1997 — covers of '60s and '70s pop hits. Rapid-fire punk reinterpretations with a humorous edge.

What kills me about the Gimmes is they have this *philosophy*. Fat Mike's liner notes are insane — he once convinced himself he was 'Fat Stevens, a guitar-toting Islamic Extremist' while they were covering pop songs, and when Spike sang 'All My Lovin'' he thought it was 'Allah my Lovin'' and condemned him to death for heresy. This is the level of committed weirdness we're talking about.

On their live album 'Ruin Jonny's Bar Mitzvah,' they played for 30 people at a Bar Mitzvah. Liner notes say 'They made a lot of mistakes. They even started a song then stopped, then started again.' They didn't fake it. They just *played*. And that's freedom right there.
The Gimmes wore matching costumes for their live shows — cowboy outfits, drag, whatever matched the album theme. They made punk rock *fun* again. Which is what we need. Fun is a weapon.

Anti-Flag. 'Die for the Government.' 1996. From A New Kind of Army on Go-Kart Records. Justin Sane and Pat Thetic formed this in Pittsburgh in 1988 — hardcore punk built on a foundation of fervent anti-war activism and criticism of U.S. foreign policy. No subtlety. No corporate compromise. Just anger at systemic disease.

In 1999, they released A New Kind of Army and it addressed everything — abortion, political corruption, racism, fascism, police brutality. Chris #2 joined the core lineup in 1999 and by 2000 they were on Warped Tour where they met Fat Mike of NOFX. That meeting built a friendship that got them on Fat Wreck Chords for Underground Network in 2001.

Here's the thing about Anti-Flag that haunts me — they signed to *RCA Records* in 2006. A major label. After spending years singing about opposition to corporate power structures. The punk community called 'selling out' so loud you could hear it from orbit. And you know what? They didn't care. They thought they could *change* the system from inside.

By 2003, they released The Terror State on Fat Wreck — *went back* to Fat Wreck — and it was entirely focused on criticizing the Bush Administration's war on terror. They were one of the few bands that actually tried to be the conscience of a nation while the nation wasn't listening.

'Die for the Government' is four minutes of distilled rage. It's the kind of song that doesn't age because the disease it's describing never gets cured. We're still here. Still singing it. Still meaning it.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide: 82 confirmed. Up 2 from this point last year. East Side holding steady. Capitol perimeter down to skeleton crew. Tucker's field reports gone dark for the last hour.
State Street Ruins quiet. Williamson St barricade still holding. Lake Mendota reports scattered — water temp holding at 33 degrees. Caroline watching for secondary cold front. Fog rolling in.

North Side reported zero activity in the last 90 minutes. Repeat: zero. Pattern continues. Reason unknown. Holden's analyzing it. Tim monitoring for signal jamming attempts — none in the last two hours.
